Notice Of Motion To Compel Arbitration (Stay Of Proceedings Pending Arbitration Also Requested)
Off Calendar Mar-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Harmeet K. Dhillon, of Dhillon Law Group Inc., Tel. 415.433.1700, for plaintiffs Encarnita Alonso;
Adrian G. Driscoll, of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel. 415.788.1900, for defendants Gurbaksh Chahal.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT GURBAKSH CHAHAL's Motion To Compel Arbitration is denied. Assuming without deciding that the sole remaining claim by plaintiff Encarnita Alonso against Mr. Chalal is arbitrable per paragraph 5 of the December 2, 2014 at-will employment letter agreement and the arbitration provisions in that letter are not unconscionable, Mr. Chalal waived his right to arbitrate. Consideration of the St. Agnes factors shows that Mr. Chalal relinquished his right to insist that this case be arbitrated. By pursuing his anti-SLAPP motion to strike in this court, Mr. Chalal acted inconsistently with his right to arbitrate and substantially invoked the "litigation machinery." Mr. Chalal also sought and received extensions on discovery propounded by Ms. Alsonso, filed two case management statements and an answer, and participated in the process of selecting a trial date without mentioning arbitration and only stated that he intended to assert his right to arbitrate after the ruling on his anti-SLAPP motion, more than sixth months after he had been served with a summons in this case. By having to oppose against the anti-SLAPP motion and participating in this case for more than six months after she served Mr. Chalal without being informed of Mr. Chalal's intent to seek arbitration, Ms. Alonso has been prejudiced because her ability to take advantage of the cost efficiencies and other benefits of arbitration have been seriously impaired, not to speak of the huge expense she incurred in opposing the anti-SLAPP motion. Most significantly, when viewed against the background of the parties' other litigation, it becomes clear that Mr. Chalal's delay in asserting his right to arbitrate was an intentional effort to try to combine what he perceived as the best of both litigation and arbitration. Case law decided since St. Agnes make clear that "a courtroom may not be used as a convenient vestibule to the arbitration hall so as to allow a party to create his own unique structure combining litigation and arbitration." (Adolph v. Coastal Auto Sales, Inc. (2010) 184 Cal.App. 4th 1443, 1452; Burton v. Cruise (2010) 190 Cal.App. 4th 939, 949; Zamora v. Lehman (2010) 186 Cal.App. 4th 1, 19; see also Lewis v. Fletcher (2012) 205 Cal.App. 4th 436, 449-53). The principles enunciated in these cases and the cases discussed therein mandate that Mr. Chalal's choice to delay seeking arbitration until after he filed and partially lost his anti-SLAPP motion be deemed a waiver of his right to arbitrate. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

Notice Of Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 425.16(C)
Off Calendar Mar-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Harmeet K. Dhillon, of Dhillon Law Group Inc., Tel. 415.433.1700, for plaintiffs Encarnita Alonso;
Adrian G. Driscoll, of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel. 415.788.1900, for defendants Gurbaksh Chahal.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT GURBAKSH CHAHAL Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 425.16(C) is granted in part. Mr. Chalal is awarded $35,913.69 against plaintiff Encarnita Alonso, calculated as $29,040 for his counsel's work on the anti-SLAPP motion, $201.69 in costs on the anti-SLAPP motion, $6672 for his counsel's work on the fees motion, and $100 in costs on the fees motion. Mr. Chalal is entitled to an award of fees and costs on both the anti-SLAPP motion and this motion because he obtained a practical benefit by striking one of two distinct defamation claims alleged against him, which has reduced the scope of the issues, discovery, potential damages, and trial preparation. The court agrees with Mr. Chalal that a 50% reduction from his reasonably incurred fees for the anti-SLAPP motion is a proper allocation for his succeeding on one of two claims on that motion. The court also agrees that his counsel's hourly rates are reasonable. The blended hourly rate for his counsel's work on the anti-SLAPP motion is $363 and for the fees motion is $278. However, the court believes that the hours spent on both of those motions (211.8 for the anti-SLAPP motion and 53.6 for the fees motion) are excessive for the issues involved and the hourly rate of counsel. There is no suggestion that any of these hours were not actually spent. Nor does the court doubt that Mr. Chalal is free to ask his counsel to spend time exceeding what is reasonably necessary to get the job done. Based on the court's involvement with the motion and another review of the motion papers, the court has determined that the maximum number of reasonable hours for the anti-SLAPP motion is 160 (4 weeks) and for the fees motion is 24 (3 days). 160 multiplied by $363 divided by two is $29,040. 24 multiplied by $278 is $6672. The costs incurred for the anti-SLAPP motion and estimated to be incurred for the fees motion are reasonable. Order signed. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Slapp Complaint
Off Calendar Jan-06-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES - ATTY. HARMEET K. DHILLON OF DLG LAW GROUP INC, TEL. NO. 1-415-433-1700 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. ADRIAN G. DRISCOLL OF THE MURPHY PEARSON BRADLEY & FEENEY, TEL. NO. 1-415-788-1900 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; THE COURT MODIFIED ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: Defendant Gurbaksch Chahal's anti-SLAPP motion to strike both causes of action in the second amended complaint is denied as to the 1st cause of action and granted as to 2nd cause of action. Mr. Chahal satisfied his first prong burden of showing that both causes of action arise from protected activity and Ms. Alonso failed to satisfy her second prong burden of showing that there is minimal merit to either of her claims. The open letter and republication of the blog post were made on websites, which are public fora. Both the open letter and the blog post were made in connection with issues of public interest, specifically asserted employment discrimination and harassment on the basis of gender and age by Silicon Valley companies and criminal charges against Mr. Chahal, and thus Ms. Alonso's defamation claims arising from the open letter and blog post arise from protected activity per CCP 425.16(e)(3) and (e)(4). Unlike the recent decision of Dual Diagnosis Treatment Center, Inc. v. Buschel (December 20, 2016) 2016 WL 7373841, the record on this motion shows that there is a direct connection between the issues of public interest and the statements that form the basis for Ms. Alonso's claims. By the publicity surrounding the filing of her employment lawsuit, Ms. Alonso became a limited purpose public figure and therefore she must provide admissible evidence that a trier of fact could find rises to the level of clear and convincing evidence that Mr. Chahal acted with actual malice in writing the open letter and republishing the blog post.
